topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the weight of a stone. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm I couldn't seem to control. I reread the text, the words blurring slightly as I tried to process the implications. The scholarship, the one I had worked so tirelessly for, was not mine. It belonged to someone else entirely. A cold sweat prickled my palms, making the keyboard slick beneath my fingers. I stood up abruptly, the cheap plastic chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. My breath hitched in my throat, shallow and fast. Every sound in the quiet dorm room seemed amplified—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant siren—and suddenly, the air felt too thin to draw into my lungs. I backed away from the screen, stumbling slightly until my back hit the cool plaster of the wall.

[story 2]

I stared at the official notification, my vision tunneling. The ink on the page seemed to swim, refusing to settle into coherent meaning. A tremor ran through my hands, making it difficult to grip my pen. This wasn't just disappointing; it was a physical shock, like being plunged into icy water. Who was the intended recipient? The name listed beside the award felt like a brand, an accusation. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action painful. My gaze darted around the small library study area, cataloging every shadow, every corner, searching for an explanation that didn't exist. My shoulders tightened up around my ears, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to squeeze the confusing reality back into a manageable shape.

[story 3]

When I finally read the final sentence, the sound seemed to drain out of the room. It was a slow, sickening drop. My entire body locked up. I felt an intense pressure building behind my sternum, making each inhalation a conscious, difficult effort. I dropped the printed document onto the desk; the paper fluttered to the floor like a dead leaf. I didn't move to pick it up. Instead, I stood rigid, my gaze fixed on the closed door of my room, as if expecting something to burst through it. A strange, hollow silence descended, punctuated only by the frantic drumming inside my own chest. I took a ragged, deep breath, but it didn't reach my lungs; it just rattled in my chest, leaving me trembling uncontrollably.

I reread the text, the words blurring slightly as I tried to process the implications. The scholarship, the one I had worked so tirelessly for, was not mine. It belonged to someone else entirely. A cold sweat prickled my palms, making the keyboard slick beneath my fingers. I stood up abruptly, the cheap plastic chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. My breath hitched in my throat, shallow and fast. Every sound in the quiet dorm room seemed amplified—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant siren—and suddenly, the air felt too thin to draw into my lungs. I backed away from the screen, stumbling slightly until my back hit the cool plaster of the wall.

I stared at the official notification, my vision tunneling. The ink on the page seemed to swim, refusing to settle into coherent meaning. A tremor ran through my hands, making it difficult to grip my pen. This wasn't just disappointing; it was a physical shock, like being plunged into icy water. Who was the intended recipient? The name listed beside the award felt like a brand, an accusation. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action painful. My gaze darted around the small library study area, cataloging every shadow, every corner, searching for an explanation that didn't exist. My shoulders tightened up around my ears, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to squeeze the confusing reality back into a manageable shape.

When I finally read the final sentence, the sound seemed to drain out of the room. It was a slow, sickening drop. My entire body locked up. I felt an intense pressure building behind my sternum, making each inhalation a conscious, difficult effort. I dropped the printed document onto the desk; the paper fluttered to the floor like a dead leaf. I didn't move to pick it up. Instead, I stood rigid, my gaze fixed on the closed door of my room, as if expecting something to burst through it. A strange, hollow silence descended, punctuated only by the frantic drumming inside my own chest. I took a ragged, deep breath, but it didn't reach my lungs; it just rattled in my chest, leaving me trembling uncontrollably.
